content.full_text:
April 22, 1994


Kathleen A. Larocque
Deputy County Counsel
County of Sonoma
575 Administration Drive, Room 118A
Santa Rosa, CA  95403-2881

                                        Re:  Your Request for Informal Assistance
                                             Our File No. I-94-137

Dear Ms. Larocque:

     This is in response to your request for assistance under the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").1/  Since you are specifically seeking informal assistance,
your letter is considered a request for informal assistance.2/

                              QUESTION

     May a county supervisor participate in a decision concerning
an application by a winery to expand the permissible use of its
property if the county supervisor's wife is a paralegal for the
law firm which represents the winery?

                              CONCLUSION

     The supervisor would not be prohibited from participating in
the decision to consider the application, provided the decision
would not have a material financial effect on his spouse's source
of income, i.e., the law firm for which she is employed.




___________________________
1/  Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections 18000-18954.  All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/  Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section
18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA  95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Advice File No. I-94-137
Page 2


FACTS

A large winery which owns facilities in Sonoma County has
filed an application with the board of supervisors to expand the
permissible use of its property. Under the proposal, the winery
would construct an additional facility on land it already owns in
the county.

The wife of an unnamed county supervisor is a paralegal in
the law firm which represents the winery on the land-use expansion
application. The supervisor's wife is an employee of the firm and
does not hold any partnership or ownership interest in the firm.
Some neighboring landowners contend that it would be a conflict of
interest for the supervisor to participate in the decision.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in, or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.

An official has a financial interest in a governmental
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the official, or on a member of the official's immediate
family,3/ or on:

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

(Section 87103(c).)

"Income" as used above includes the community property
interest in the income of a spouse. (Section 82030.)

Accordingly, the supervisor may not make, participate in
making, or use his official position to influence a governmental

3/ An official's "immediate family" includes his spouse and
dependent children. (Section 82029.)

Advice File No. I-94-137
Page 3


decision if it is reasonably foreseeable4/ that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect
on the public generally, on his sources of income, including the
law firm for which his wife is employed.

The Commission has developed regulations to determine when a
particular decision would have a material financial effect on a
source of income. Regulation 18702.1 provides that when a source
of income is "directly" before the public official, the decision
is deemed to have a material financial effect on the source. A
person or business entity is directly before an official when it:

     (1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision
     will be made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or
     similar request or;

     (2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the
     proceeding concerning the decision before the official
     or the official's agency.

     (3) A person or business entity is the subject of
     a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance,
     renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
     permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the
     subject person or business entity.

                              (Regulation 18702.1(b).)


The decision before the board of supervisors was initiated by
the winery as the applicant for a request for a expansion on the
permissible use of their property. The law firm is not the
applicant in the proceeding nor is it the subject of the decision.
Instead, the law firm is acting in a representative capacity.
Thus, the decision before the board of supervisors will not have a
"direct" affect on the law firm.

The county supervisor may still have a conflict of interest
if a decision could have an "indirect" affect on the law firm.
For example, as a result of the decision, if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the law firm would engage in additional business
from the winery relative to the application to expand the
permissible use of the property, the supervisor would not be able
to participate in that decision, if the decision would have a
material financial effect on the law firm.

4/ Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

Advice File No. I-94-137
Page 4


For business entities which will be "indirectly" affected by
a decision, the Commission has developed a regulation which sets
out monetary thresholds which must affect the entity's income,
assets or liabilities, before a decision is considered to have a
material financial effect. The monetary effect on the business
differs depending on the fiscal size of the entity. For example,
for the smallest business entity on the scale, the decision must
increase or decrease gross revenues for a fiscal year by $30,000
or more; result in the business entity incurring or avoiding
additional expenses for a fiscal year by $2,500 or more; or result
in an increase or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of $10,000 or more. (Regulation 18702.2(g).)

You have not provided any information on the fiscal size of
the law firm to enable us to apply the appropriate standard. A
copy of Regulation 18702.2 is enclosed for your reference.

Accordingly, if you have determined that it is reasonably
foreseeable that the law firm will generate additional business
from the winery client as a result of this decision, and the
increase will be material under Regulation 18702.2, the supervisor
may not participate.

If you have any additional questions, or wish to discuss
this, please feel free to contact this office at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division

SGC/jt
Enclosure
